annotations.label: Cartesian coordinate system

anchor.snippet: specifies each point uniquely by a pair of real numbers called coordinates

annotations.note: Mathlib provides orthonormal bases of Euclidean space that give coordinate representations, but no concept named 'Cartesian coordinate system'.

annotations.label: Origin

anchor.snippet: The point where the axes meet is called the origin and has (0, 0) as coordinates.

annotations.note: The origin is just the zero vector of the space; there is no separately named 'origin' declaration.

annotations.label: Cartesian frame

anchor.snippet: The combination of origin and basis forms a coordinate frame called the Cartesian frame .

annotations.note: AffineBasis captures a base point plus an associated basis but is not restricted to orthonormal Cartesian frames.

annotations.label: Three-dimensional Cartesian coordinates

anchor.snippet: the position of any point in three-dimensional space can be specified by three Cartesian coordinates

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3) is exactly three-dimensional Euclidean space with Cartesian coordinates.

annotations.label: n-dimensional Cartesian coordinates

anchor.snippet: Cartesian coordinates specify the point in an n -dimensional Euclidean space

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n) is the n-dimensional Euclidean space with Cartesian coordinate functions.

annotations.label: Circle of radius 2 as an equation

anchor.snippet: a circle of radius 2, centered at the origin of the plane, may be described as the set of all points

annotations.note: Metric.sphere 0 2 in EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) is this set, but the specific example is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Number line

anchor.snippet: An affine line with a chosen Cartesian coordinate system is called a number line .

annotations.note: ℝ exists but there is no named 'number line' / coordinatized-affine-line concept.

annotations.label: Two degrees of freedom on a line

anchor.snippet: There are two degrees of freedom in the choice of Cartesian coordinate system for a line

annotations.note: No declaration counts the degrees of freedom in choosing a coordinate system on a line.

annotations.label: Line transformation as real function

anchor.snippet: A geometric transformation of the line can be represented by a function of a real variable

annotations.note: There is no formalized statement representing geometric transformations of a line as real functions.

annotations.label: Coordinate systems related by linear function

anchor.snippet: Any two Cartesian coordinate systems on the line can be related to each-other by a linear function

annotations.note: AffineEquiv formalizes the affine maps relating coordinate systems, but no lemma asserts this for two coordinatizations of a line.

annotations.label: Affine map between coordinatized lines

anchor.snippet: Choosing a coordinate system for each of two different lines establishes an affine map from one line to the other

annotations.note: AffineMap formalizes affine maps between affine spaces, but this construction from chosen coordinate systems is not stated.

annotations.label: Cartesian coordinate system in two dimensions

anchor.snippet: is defined by an ordered pair of perpendicular lines (axes)

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) with its standard orthonormal basis is the two-dimensional Cartesian system.

annotations.label: Abscissa and ordinate

anchor.snippet: The first and second coordinates are called the abscissa and the ordinate of P

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named 'abscissa'/'ordinate' for the coordinate components.

annotations.label: Cartesian plane

anchor.snippet: A Euclidean plane with a chosen Cartesian coordinate system is called a Cartesian plane .

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) is the Euclidean/Cartesian plane with chosen coordinates.

annotations.label: Quadrants

anchor.snippet: The two axes divide the plane into four right angles , called quadrants .

annotations.note: There is no general 'quadrant' decomposition of the coordinate plane in Mathlib (only ad hoc complex-analysis quadrant regions).

annotations.label: Distances of a point from the axes

anchor.snippet: its distances from the X -axis and from the Y -axis are

annotations.note: No lemma states that a point's distances from the coordinate axes equal the absolute values of its coordinates.

annotations.label: Cartesian coordinate system in three dimensions

anchor.snippet: A Cartesian coordinate system for a three-dimensional space consists of an ordered triplet of lines

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3) with its standard orthonormal basis is the three-dimensional Cartesian system.

annotations.label: Coordinate as distance to opposite plane

anchor.snippet: each coordinate of a point P can be taken as the distance from P to the plane defined by the other two axes

annotations.note: No lemma identifies a coordinate with the signed distance to the plane of the other two axes.

annotations.label: Coordinate planes and octants

anchor.snippet: Each pair of axes defines a coordinate plane .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named coordinate-plane or octant decomposition of 3-space.

annotations.label: Abscissa, ordinate, applicate

anchor.snippet: Standard names for the coordinates in the three axes are abscissa , ordinate and applicate .

annotations.note: These coordinate names are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Right-hand rule orientation (3D)

anchor.snippet: The orientation is usually chosen so that the 90-degree angle from the first axis to the second axis looks counter-clockwise

annotations.note: Orientation formalizes the abstract choice of orientation, but the geometric right-hand-rule convention itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Identification with Cartesian product

anchor.snippet: the points of a Cartesian plane can be identified with pairs of real numbers

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.equiv gives the (continuous-linear) identification EuclideanSpace 𝕜 ι ≃ (ι → 𝕜), specializing to pairs for Fin 2.

annotations.label: Oblique coordinate system

anchor.snippet: generalizes to allow axes that are not perpendicular to each other

annotations.note: A general (non-orthonormal) Module.Basis provides oblique coordinates, but oblique systems are not separately named.

annotations.label: Quadrants numbered I–IV

anchor.snippet: divide the plane into four infinite regions, called quadrants

annotations.note: The numbered quadrant regions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Octants

anchor.snippet: a three-dimensional Cartesian system defines a division of space into eight regions or octants

annotations.note: The eight octants of 3-space are not formalized.

annotations.label: Orthant

anchor.snippet: The generalization of the quadrant and octant to an arbitrary number of dimensions is the orthant

annotations.note: There is no named 'orthant' concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in the plane

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean distance between two points of the plane with Cartesian coordinates

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq gives dist x y = √(∑ (xᵢ-yᵢ)²), which specializes to the planar distance formula for Fin 2.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in space

anchor.snippet: In three-dimensional space, the distance between points

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq gives the general √(∑ (xᵢ-yᵢ)²) formula, specializing to 3D distance for Fin 3.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem (Cartesian form)

anchor.snippet: This is the Cartesian version of Pythagoras's theorem .

annotations.note: The Pythagorean theorem in Euclidean geometry is formalized; the Cartesian distance formula is its coordinate expression via EuclideanSpace.dist_eq.

annotations.label: Euclidean transformations

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean transformations or Euclidean motions are the ( bijective ) mappings of points of the Euclidean plane to themselves

annotations.note: IsometryEquiv is the bijective distance-preserving map, of which Euclidean plane motions are the case on EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2).

annotations.label: Four types of isometries

anchor.snippet: There are four types of these mappings (also called isometries)

annotations.note: The classification of plane isometries into translation, rotation, reflection, and glide reflection is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Translation of coordinates

anchor.snippet: is equivalent to adding a fixed pair of numbers ( a , b ) to the Cartesian coordinates of every point in the set

annotations.note: AffineEquiv.constVAdd v is translation by a fixed vector, matching coordinatewise addition of (a,b).

annotations.label: Rotation about the origin

anchor.snippet: To rotate a figure counterclockwise around the origin by some angle

annotations.note: Orientation.rotation is rotation by an oriented angle in an oriented 2D inner product space, matching the cos/sin formula.

annotations.label: Reflection across coordinate axes

anchor.snippet: then (− x , y ) are the coordinates of its reflection across the second coordinate axis

annotations.note: Submodule.reflection in a hyperplane specializes to axis reflections, but the coordinate formula (x,y)↦(-x,y) is not a stated lemma.

annotations.label: Reflection across line through origin at angle

anchor.snippet: reflection across a line through the origin making an angle

annotations.note: Submodule.reflection in a 1-dim subspace covers this geometrically; the explicit (cos 2θ, sin 2θ; sin 2θ, -cos 2θ) coordinate formula is not stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Glide reflection

anchor.snippet: A glide reflection is the composition of a reflection across a line followed by a translation in the direction of that line.

annotations.note: Glide reflection is not formalized as a named transformation.

annotations.label: Affine transformations via matrices

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: All affine transformations of the plane can be described in a uniform way by using matrices.

annotations.note: AffineMap stores a linear part plus translation, but the augmented-matrix representation is not a named declaration.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: All affine transformations of the plane can be described in a uniform way by using matrices.

annotations.label: Euclidean transformations have orthogonal matrix

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: the Euclidean transformations are characterized by the fact that the matrix

annotations.note: Change-of-orthonormal-basis matrices lie in the orthogonal group, but the iff characterization of plane Euclidean transformations is not stated.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: the Euclidean transformations are characterized by the fact that the matrix

annotations.label: Translation iff A is identity

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: The transformation is a translation if and only if A is the identity matrix .

annotations.note: Translations are exactly affine maps with identity linear part, but this iff is not stated as a lemma.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: The transformation is a translation if and only if A is the identity matrix .

annotations.label: Rotation iff A is rotation matrix

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: The transformation is a rotation around some point if and only if A is a rotation matrix

annotations.note: SO(n) is formalized as specialOrthogonalGroup, but the geometric iff for rotations about a point is not stated.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: The transformation is a rotation around some point if and only if A is a rotation matrix

annotations.label: Reflection or glide reflection condition

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: A reflection or glide reflection is obtained when

annotations.note: The det A = -1 classification into reflections/glide reflections is not formalized.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: A reflection or glide reflection is obtained when

annotations.label: Composition by multiplying matrices

anchor.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchor.snippet: transformations can be composed by simply multiplying the associated transformation matrices

annotations.note: LinearMap.toMatrix_comp states that the matrix of a composition equals the product of the matrices.

anchors.section: General matrix form of the transformations
anchors.snippet: transformations can be composed by simply multiplying the associated transformation matrices

annotations.label: Affine transformations of the plane

anchor.snippet: Affine transformations of the Euclidean plane are transformations that map lines to lines

annotations.note: AffineMap formalizes affine maps (defined via affine combinations), an equivalent characterization to the line-preserving one.

anchors.snippet: Affine transformations of the Euclidean plane are transformations that map lines to lines

annotations.label: Euclidean as affine with orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean transformations are the affine transformations such that the 2×2 matrix of the

annotations.note: AffineIsometryEquiv is exactly an affine equivalence whose linear part is an isometry (orthogonal matrix), capturing Euclidean transformations.

annotations.label: Composition of affine transformations

anchor.snippet: The augmented matrix that represents the composition of two affine transformations is obtained by multiplying their augmented matrices.

annotations.note: AffineMap.comp formalizes composition of affine maps, but the augmented-matrix-product statement is not given.

annotations.label: Scaling

anchor.snippet: An example of an affine transformation which is not Euclidean is given by scaling.

annotations.note: AffineMap.homothety is uniform scaling about a center; general per-axis scaling is via diagonal linear maps.

annotations.label: Shearing

anchor.snippet: A shearing transformation will push the top of a square sideways to form a parallelogram

annotations.note: Matrix.transvection (the identity plus c in an off-diagonal entry) is exactly a shear matrix.

annotations.label: Orientation (handedness) of the plane

anchor.snippet: Each of these two choices determines a different orientation (also called handedness ) of the Cartesian plane.

annotations.note: Orientation formalizes the two possible orientations of a real vector space (exactly two for nonzero finite dimension).

annotations.label: Standard right-handed orientation

anchor.snippet: is considered the positive or standard orientation, also called the right-handed orientation

annotations.note: Module.Oriented.positiveOrientation is the standard/positive orientation determined by the standard basis.

annotations.label: Rotation preserves orientation

anchor.snippet: rotating the coordinate system will preserve the orientation

annotations.note: Orientation and orientation-preserving maps are formalized, but no lemma directly states that rotations preserve orientation.

annotations.label: Right- and left-handed systems (3D)

anchor.snippet: The two possible coordinate systems which result are called 'right-handed' and 'left-handed'.

annotations.note: Orientation captures the two orientation classes (right- vs left-handed) of a 3D space, though the right-hand-rule labelling is not formalized.

annotations.label: Position vector

anchor.section: Representing a vector in the standard basis
anchor.snippet: A point in space in a Cartesian coordinate system may also be represented by a position vector

annotations.note: In EuclideanSpace points are themselves vectors, so the position-vector representation is built in but is not a separately named concept.

annotations.label: Standard basis

anchor.section: Representing a vector in the standard basis
anchor.snippet: are unit vectors in the direction of the x -axis and y -axis respectively, generally referred to as the standard basis

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.basisFun is the standard orthonormal basis of unit coordinate vectors (i, j, ...).

anchors.section: Representing a vector in the standard basis
anchors.snippet: are unit vectors in the direction of the x -axis and y -axis respectively, generally referred to as the standard basis

annotations.label: Identification with complex numbers

anchor.section: Representing a vector in the standard basis
anchor.snippet: identify the point with coordinates ( x , y ) with the complex number z = x + iy

annotations.note: Complex.equivRealProd is the equivalence ℂ ≃ ℝ × ℝ identifying z = x + iy with (x, y).

annotations.label: Identification with quaternions (3D)

anchor.section: Representing a vector in the standard basis
anchor.snippet: In a three-dimensional cartesian space a similar identification can be made with a subset of the quaternions

annotations.note: The pure-imaginary quaternions (Quaternion.im) form the 3D subspace identified with ℝ³; multiplication restricted there gives the cross-product/scalar-product decomposition.
